reasoning: The statistical linear model gives a 30% baseline probability of an increase. However, the macroeconomic environment fundamentally points downward: the FOMC has been cutting rates and is broadly expected to continue cutting (with median projections of 75bps of cuts in 2025). The national average savings rate (SNDR) tends to lag but ultimately follow the Fed funds rate. While there was a minor bump from 0.38 to 0.40 recently, high-yield savings accounts will be lowering their yields in response to Fed cuts, pulling the weighted average down or keeping it flat at best. For the rate to strictly increase above 0.40 by December 9, 2025 (only one month ahead), the chance is extremely low given the dominant downward momentum for yields — estimated at 15%. For the February 7, 2026 resolution date, further rate cuts are expected to apply continued downward pressure, though random fluctuations in deposit re-weighting or individual bank policies slightly raise the probability of a random positive print above 0.40. Following the principle of increasing uncertainty over longer time horizons, the probability moves closer to 0.5, estimated at 25%.
